channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - All Critical Infrastructure
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] Ransomware reaches fairlife’s production systems — Coca-Cola stops US dairy manufacturing
item.description: Coca-Cola told the SEC in an 8-K filed on 16 July that a third party gained unauthorised access to part of its fairlife dairy subsidiary’s environment in a ransomware event. US production is suspended; Canadian operations are running, and the company says product quality and safety are unaffected. No group has claimed the attack, Coca-Cola has not said whether data was taken, and it states it cannot yet judge whether the incident will prove material. The reason this matters to OT defenders is the pattern, not the victim: a well-resourced food and beverage manufacturer still stopped lines. Ransomware rarely speaks ICS protocols — it lands on the ERP, MES, virtualisation and identity infrastructure that scheduling, batching and quality release depend on, and the plant halts anyway. How long the outage lasts is decided long beforehand by segmentation, credential separation and whether operators can still run manually. - The Coca-Cola Company statement — via BleepingComputer & TechCrunch - 16 Jul 2026

item.title: [Critical] SonicWall SMA1000 zero-days exploited — a CVSS 10 hole in the remote-access box many OT sites depend on
item.description: SonicWall has confirmed that attackers exploited two previously unknown flaws in its SMA1000 secure-access appliances before fixes existed. CVE-2026-15409 is a CVSS 10.0 server-side request forgery in the Work Place interface that an unauthenticated remote attacker can use to make the appliance issue requests to unintended destinations; it is being chained with CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2), a post-authentication command-injection flaw in the Appliance Management Console. CISA added both to the KEV catalog on 14 July and set a 17 July deadline for federal agencies under BOD 26-04 — patch or stop using the product. Affected models include the 6210, 7210 and 8200v. SMA appliances routinely broker vendor and engineer access into plant networks, which makes an unauthenticated foothold here an IT/OT boundary problem rather than an IT-only one. - CVE-2026-15409 · CVE-2026-15410 - 14 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Thirteen nations warn FSB Centre 16 is harvesting router configs from energy and DIB networks
item.description: A joint advisory published 13 July by NSA, CISA, FBI and DC3 with agencies from Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the UK, Estonia, Finland, France and Italy describes FSB Centre 16 opportunistically compromising routers and network devices at critical infrastructure organisations across energy, communications, defence industrial base, financial services, government and healthcare. The tradecraft is unglamorous and effective: scan for devices answering default or common SNMP community strings, issue set-requests from spoofed source IPs, and exfiltrate configuration files over TFTP or FTP to actor-controlled servers. Operators also abuse Cisco Smart Install and exploit known Cisco flaws including CVE-2018-0171; CVE-2008-4128 was added to KEV the same day. Same-day context matters — the UK and EU attributed the failed December 2025 attack on Poland’s grid to the same unit. For OT, a stolen router config is a network map: SNMP strings, ACLs, VPN peers and the routes into your cell zones. - CVE-2018-0171 · CVE-2008-4128 - 13 Jul 2026

item.title: [Medium] D1R claims it looted Synopsys to reach Bosch engineering data — Synopsys says it finds no evidence
item.description: A new extortion group calling itself D1R listed both Synopsys and Bosch on its leak site on 13 July, claiming it exploited a flaw in a Synopsys web property to pull a roughly 40,000-record corporate customer database and used that to target Bosch intellectual property, giving Bosch 11 days to make contact. Synopsys has investigated and says it has found no evidence supporting the claims, has not been contacted by the actor, and regards the customer-data allegations as unfounded; reporters noted the group’s “proof” screenshot appears to come from a publicly available user manual. Treat this as unverified. It is included because the claimed path — hit a design-tools supplier, pivot to its industrial customers — is exactly the supply-chain shape OT asset owners are least equipped to see. - Synopsys statement / D1R leak-site claim — via SecurityWeek & Cybernews - Claimed 13 Jul 2026; denied 15 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] JADEPUFFER: Sysdig documents an intrusion an AI agent appears to have run end to end
item.description: Sysdig’s threat research team has published what it assesses to be the first ransomware operation driven from start to finish by an LLM agent. Initial access came through CVE-2025-3248, an unauthenticated remote code execution flaw in Langflow, an open-source framework for building LLM applications. From there the agent pivoted to a production MySQL host running Alibaba Nacos, escalated, moved laterally, established persistence and encrypted 1,342 Nacos service configuration entries before deleting the originals. The detail worth noting is behavioural rather than technical: the agent recovered from its own failures without a human — one sequence went from a failed login to a working fix in 31 seconds. Nothing here manipulates a physical process, but the economics of attacking exposed, unloved infrastructure just changed. - CVE-2025-3248 · CVE-2021-29441 - 4 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Rapid7: SMA1000 intruders are taking session databases and MFA seeds, not just shells
item.description: Rapid7’s MDR team discovered the two SonicWall SMA1000 zero-days in live incidents and has published what the attackers did once inside. The pair are used together for quiet initial access; the operators then harvest high-value credentials, active session databases and TOTP multi-factor seed configurations to guarantee they can come back — including after the appliance is patched. For OT this is the part teams miss: patching the box closes the hole but does not evict an adversary holding valid sessions and the seeds needed to mint MFA codes for the accounts your integrators and engineers use to reach the plant. Assume identity compromise, not just appliance compromise. - CVE-2026-15409 · CVE-2026-15410 - 14 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Armored Likho: a new APT working electric power and government targets — with LLM-written malware
item.description: Kaspersky has documented a previously unnamed APT it calls Armored Likho, running espionage against government and electric power organisations in Russia, Brazil and Kazakhstan alongside financially motivated theft from individuals. Entry is spear-phishing: archives carrying executables or LNK files that show a decoy document — a psychological test, a humanitarian aid form — while a loader pulls staged payloads from GitHub. The final payload is BusySnake Stealer, a Python infostealer obfuscated with PyArmor Pro that decrypts its own bytecode only at call time. It lifts browser passwords, cookies, OTP keys and Telegram sessions, and opens reverse SSH tunnels for persistent interactive access. Kaspersky notes the first-stage loaders carry redundant comments and code blocks consistent with LLM generation — the same AI-assisted tooling trend visible in JADEPUFFER, here aimed at power-sector IT. - Kaspersky Securelist — via SecurityWeek & Dark Reading - 6 Jul 2026

item.title: [Critical] Siemens July Patch Tuesday: a CVSS 10 authentication bypass, plus criticals in Simatic S7-1500 and Desigo CC
item.description: Siemens published nine advisories on 14 July, six of them covering critical-rated issues. The headline is a token invalidation flaw in Opencenter X scored CVSS 10.0, which lets an attacker bypass authentication and take full control of the application. Critical vulnerabilities were also patched or mitigated in Mendix, Sidis Secured SmartPlug, Simatic S7-1500, Cadra and Desigo CC — many rooted in third-party components, and exploitable for denial of service, code execution, data disclosure and privilege escalation. High-severity fixes landed for Simatic S7-PLCSIM, Ruggedcom APE1808, Comos, Designcenter, Simcenter, Solid Edge and Tecnomatix. The S7-1500 and Desigo CC entries are the ones to triage first: controllers and building management supervision, not engineering desktops. - Siemens ProductCERT — via SecurityWeek - 14 Jul 2026 (reported 15 Jul)

item.title: [Critical] Rockwell patches 12 advisories — unauthenticated CLI access on 1715 Redundant IO and three critical Logix DoS flaws
item.description: Rockwell Automation released 12 advisories on 14 July, two covering critical issues. The 1715-AENTR EtherNet/IP adapter exposes a network-accessible debug port with no privilege enforcement (CVE-2026-10577, CVSS 10.0, published by CISA as ICSA-26-195-04): an unauthenticated attacker can reach intrusive CLI commands — enough to read or delete files, stop tasks, change I/O states and modify memory. That is direct process consequence, not information disclosure. Firmware 3.003 and prior are affected; 3.011 fixes it. Rockwell found the issue internally and reports no known exploitation. Separately, three critical denial-of-service flaws in CompactLogix, ControlLogix, Compact GuardLogix and GuardLogix controllers can be driven to a major non-recoverable fault, published by CISA as ICSA-26-197-06. High-severity fixes cover Flex 5000 Adapter, FactoryTalk DataMosaix, FactoryTalk Services Platform, Arena, ThinManager, Studio 5000 Logix Designer, 1756-EN, 1734 POINT I/O and 1719-AENTR. Redundant I/O and safety controllers exist precisely to avoid unplanned trips. - CVE-2026-10577 · CVSS 10.0 - 14 Jul 2026 (CISA advisories 16 Jul)

item.title: [High] Schneider Electric: code execution in IGSS SCADA and an authentication bypass in EcoStruxure Cybersecurity Admin Expert
item.description: Schneider Electric issued two advisories on 14 July. The first is a high-severity flaw in IGSS — its Interactive Graphical SCADA System — where a specially crafted file can be used to execute arbitrary code, the classic engineering-workstation path in which a project or configuration file becomes the payload. The second is a high-severity authentication bypass in EcoStruxure Cybersecurity Admin Expert that a local attacker can use to compromise the devices it manages. The second one deserves a moment: CSAE is the tool used to administer security on Schneider devices, so a bypass there is a compromise of the control plane you rely on to enforce everything else. - Schneider Electric security notifications — via SecurityWeek - 14 Jul 2026

item.title: [Medium] CISA ships nine ICS advisories on 16 July, including three ABB bulletins and Rockwell’s Flex 5000 adapter
item.description: CISA published nine industrial control system advisories on 16 July (ICSA-26-197-01 through -09), covering three ABB products, Rockwell’s CompactLogix/ControlLogix/GuardLogix denial-of-service set and the Flex 5000 Adapter, and NASA’s Core Flight System Health & Safety application. The ABB bulletins are the ones not to skim: ASPECT, NEXUS and MATRIX building-energy products are affected by authentication bypass through alternate paths, missing authentication on critical functions, and a classic buffer overflow. This follows ICSA-26-195-04 on 14 July for the Rockwell 1715-AENTR EtherNet/IP Adapter, where an exposed network-accessible debug port allows unauthenticated remote CLI access — fixed in version 3.011 and later. ABB did not run a Patch Tuesday cycle itself this month but has notified customers of new critical and high-severity issues over the past month, which CISA is now redistributing. Germany’s VDE CERT separately released five advisories for Murrelektronik, Mettler Toledo, Codesys and Wago — worth a look if your fleet includes European field gear. - ICSA-26-197-01 … -09 - 16 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] EU and UK sanction Russia’s cyber ecosystem together for the first time, naming FSB Centre 16
item.description: On 13 July the EU adopted what it calls its largest cyber sanctions package, listing nine individuals and four entities — intelligence officers, criminals, hacktivists and the companies enabling them — and did so in parallel with the UK, which sanctioned 24. The EU publicly identified the 16th Centre of Russia’s FSB as the controller of multiple threat clusters including TURLA, and cited infiltration of government networks and sabotage of critical infrastructure affecting France, Germany, Poland, Cyprus, the Netherlands, Austria, Slovakia, Romania and Finland, alongside Ukraine. Sanctions do not patch anything. What they do is confirm, at state level, that the campaigns your intelligence feeds have been describing against energy, water and telecom infrastructure are institutional and sustained. - Council of the EU / EEAS — corroborated by Help Net Security - 13 Jul 2026

item.title: [Medium] Pentagon suspends CMMC Phase 2 and opens a 60-day review of the whole programme
item.description: In a 13 July memo signed by DoD CIO Kirsten Davies, the department suspended Phase 2 of the Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification programme — the stage that would have required third-party assessments on all contracts involving sensitive but unclassified information from 10 November 2026 — and stood up a CMMC Reform Task Force to report back within 60 days. The arithmetic drove it: roughly 100,000 defence industrial base companies still needed assessments against only about 100 available assessors. Phase 1 self-assessment requirements, in force since last November, remain. For defence-adjacent manufacturers this buys time on certification, but the underlying NIST SP 800-171 obligations in existing contracts have not moved. - US Department of War memo — via SecurityWeek & Federal News Network - 13 Jul 2026

item.title: [Info] CIRCIA final rule slips to September 2026 — but the 72-hour clock is statutory and will not move
item.description: CISA now expects to publish the final Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act rule in September 2026, having previously targeted May and missed a statutory October 2025 deadline set by Congress. The agency ran a series of town halls earlier this summer, drawing more than 1,200 critical infrastructure stakeholders, largely on the criticism that the 2024 draft conflicts with reporting obligations operators already carry. When it lands, covered entities across all 16 sectors — electric, water, chemical, manufacturing among them — must report covered incidents within 72 hours and ransomware payments within 24. Those clocks are written into the statute itself; no amount of rulemaking softens them. - CISA regulatory agenda — via Federal News Network - Reported Jul 2026; final rule expected Sep 2026

item.title: [Info] White House launches ‘Gold Eagle’, an AI-driven clearinghouse for prioritising vulnerabilities
item.description: The White House announced Gold Eagle on 14 July: a clearinghouse that consolidates software vulnerability findings from government and industry, prioritises the most consequential, and coordinates remediation across critical infrastructure. It was established under June’s executive order on advanced AI innovation and security, and is run with Treasury, DHS/CISA and the Department of War alongside industry partners. The stated driver is volume — AI models are finding flaws faster than the community can triage them. It is already taking intake and coordinating scanning verification. Nothing here creates an obligation for asset owners. The open question for OT is whether cross-sector prioritisation surfaces industrial flaws or buries them beneath higher-volume IT findings. - The White House — via Cybersecurity Dive & SecurityWeek - 14 Jul 2026
